subject: critical-care
title: Care of the critically ill patient
preambleMarkdown:
# Care of the critically ill patient



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Care of the critically ill patient is a clinical condition or syndrome in critical care.
- This article focuses on rapid stabilization, organ support, invasive monitoring, source control, and ICU complications.
- The practical clinical priority is critical care diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Care of the critically ill patient to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Care of the critically ill patient is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is critical care diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Care of the critically ill patient depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In critical care, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Care of the critically ill patient.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Care of the critically ill patient disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Care of the critically ill patient should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Care of the critically ill patient first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused critical care examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Care of the critically ill patient presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Care of the critically ill patient with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Care of the critically ill patient is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Care of the critically ill patient should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Care of the critically ill patient is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses critical care diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Care of the critically ill patient may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Care of the critically ill patient patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Care of the critically ill patient in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Care of the critically ill patient does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Care of the critically ill patient may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Care of the critically ill patient, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Care of the critically ill patient as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Care of the critically ill patient draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

sections.heading: Imported Source Notes
sections.bodyMarkdown:

### Source Excerpt: Approach to the Patient

_Source lines: 335-401._

```text
II. APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
A. Primary survey. A rapid (10-second) assessment of the ABCs
1. Circulation is assessed by palpating either a carotid or femoral pulse. If no
pulse is palpable, chest compressions should be performed to promote blood
flow until a defibrillator is available. Cardiac rhythm should be assessed imme-
diately with the defibrillator paddles to identify ventricular fibrillation (VF) or
another rhythm responsive to cardioversion (see Clinical Pearl 1-2).

2. Airway and breathing are assessed by visualizing spontaneous respirations
while hearing or feeling expired air from the patient's airway. Head tilt and chin
lift procedures may be performed for patients without risk of cervical spine in-
jury. If spontaneous respirations are not present, ventilation should be assisted
by mouth-to-mask or bag-valve-mask breathing.

3. Secondary survey. More definitive management of the ABCs and investigating
the underlying cause
a. Airway. The decision to perform endotracheal intubation or use other
airway adjuncts to maintain and protect the airway must be made.

b. Breathing. The administration of oxygen and assessment of need for
ventilatory support with positive pressure
c. Circulation
i. Intravenous access should be obtained for the delivery of fluids and
medications required for resuscitation. Standard intravenous access for
resuscitation is two 14- or 16-gauge peripheral intravenous catheters.

Intraosseous or central venous access may be obtained.

ii. Electrocardiographic monitoring should be instituted, and the cardiac
rhythm identified and treated using ACLS guidelines. The underlying
cause of the arrest should be identified and treated if possible.

d. Other interventions
i. Laboratory studies including hemoglobin, electrolytes, and serum and
urine toxicology should be considered.

ii. Bladder catheterization with a Foley catheter should be considered to
assist in fluid management.

iii. Nasogastric tube placement should be considered for patients who are
being mechanically ventilated (to decrease aspiration risk). Nasogastric
tube placement should also be considered for patients suspected of
drug overdose (to allow administration of decontamination agents).

4. Reassessment. The patient should be reassessed frequently using both the
primary and secondary surveys until stabilized.

Normal ventilatory control is mediated by the:
- Central and peripheral chemoreceptors, which detect changes in the pH and arterial oxygen tension
(PO2), respectively
- Respiratory control center (brain stem-integrating and motor neurons)
- Respiration effectors
- Neuromuscular (spinal cord, nerves, muscles)
- Ventilatory (chest wall, pleura, airways, lung parenchyma)
PEARL	CLINICAL 	1-1
Early defibrillation (direct current [DC] cardioversion) is the most important intervention for successful resusci-
tation during known VF cardiac arrest and should take precedence over intravenous line placement and intu-
bation. In a witnessed arrest, the initial therapy should be application of an automated external defibrillator
as soon as one is available.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	1-2
2 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Diseases of the Cardiovascular System
Resuscitation
```

### Source Excerpt: Approach to the Patient

_Source lines: 36274-36531._

```text
I. APPROACH TO THE PATIENT
A. Initial stabilization. As with any other patient in the emergency department (ED),
initial stabilization of the poisoned patient involves assessment of airway, breath-
ing, and circulation (the ABCs).

1. Airway. Check for the presence of a gag reflex and assess the need for intuba-
tion, initially and serially, over the period of observation. Causes of airway
compromise include:
a. Posterior displacement of the tongue (e.g., from central nervous system
[CNS] and respiratory depressants)
b. Oropharyngeal mucosal injury or edema (e.g., from caustic ingestions)
c. Angioedema (e.g., from angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors)
d. Trauma
2. Breathing. Assess the adequacy of oxygenation and ventilation with pulse
oximetry and arterial blood gas (ABG) determinations. Breathing may be
compromised by any of the following:
a. Hypoventilation (e.g., from CNS or respiratory depressants, peripheral
muscle toxins)
b. Aspiration (e.g., from CNS or respiratory depressants, peripheral muscle
toxins)
c. Pulmonary edema (e.g., from inhalational injuries, heroin, salicylates)
3. Circulation. Assess the heart rate and rhythm, the blood pressure, and the
adequacy of perfusion. Circulation may be compromised by a multitude of
medications and toxins.

B. Patient history (see Clinical Pearl 20-1). The patient may not be cooperative or
may be unable to give an accurate history of ingestion or exposure. Other sources
of information include family members, friends, coworkers, rescue personnel, the
patient's physician or pharmacist, and old hospital records.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	20-1
Questions to ask include the following:
1. What?

a. What drugs is the patient taking?

b. What drugs or chemicals are available to the patient?

c. What chemicals or toxins is the patient exposed to at work?

d. What (e.g., pill bottles, chemical containers, drug paraphernalia) was present at the scene?

e. What events have occurred since the ingestion or exposure?

2. How much?

a. How much of the drug or chemical was initially available?

b. How much of the drug or chemical is remaining in the bottle or container?

3. When?

a. When was the patient last observed to be at his or her baseline?

b. When did the patient ingest or become exposed to the drug, chemical, or toxin?

Toxicologic Emergencies
C. Physical examination
1. Vital signs should be assessed and managed as appropriate.

2. Neurologic findings. During the neurologic examination, the level of con-
sciousness, pupil size, and reactivity should be assessed, and focal neurologic
abnormalities should be noted.

3. "Toxidromes" are physical signs of toxicologic syndromes that may aid in the
diagnosis (see Clinical Pearl 20-2).

4. Other findings. Evidence of trauma (e.g., head trauma) or a medical disorder
(e.g., hypo- or hyperglycemia, hypothyroidism) should be sought.

D. Administration of the "coma cocktail"
1. Thiamine. Malnutrition or alcoholism predisposes the patient to thiamine
deficiency. Patients suspected of having Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome
(see Chapter 8.I.E.1) should receive thiamine, 1 g intravenously. To prevent the de-
velopment of Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome in alcoholic patients, thiamine (100 mg
intravenously) should be administered as proximate in time to dextrose as possible.

2. Dextrose. As is the case with any patient with an altered mental status, the
serum glucose should be assessed by rapid bedside testing. Hypoglycemia is
treated with intravenous dextrose.

3. Naloxone. If an opioid toxidrome exists, or the patient's mental status is con-
sistent with opiate ingestion, an initial dose of naloxone (0.01 mg/kg intrave-
nously) is administered.

a. Doses as high as 10 mg or more may be required to reverse respiratory de-
pression, and patients who have ingested certain types of synthetic opioids
may require large or repeated doses of naloxone.

Quick 	HIT
The "coma cocktail" consists
of a group of antidotes that
may be of value, both diag-
nostically and therapeutically,
during the initial assessment
and treatment of patients
with altered mental status.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	20-2
Toxidromes
1. Sympathomimetic "toxidrome"
a. Hyperthermia
b. Tachycardia
c. Hypertension
d. Dilated pupils
e. Warm, moist skin
f. Altered mental status (e.g., agitation, hallucinations, combativeness) and seizures
2. Cholinergic "toxidrome"
a. Profuse salivation
b. Bradycardia or tachycardia
c. Pinpoint pupils
d. Diaphoresis
e. Excessive bronchial secretions and bronchospasm
f. Hyperactive bowel sounds
g. Urinary or fecal incontinence (or both)
h. Muscle fasciculations and weakness
i. Altered mental status and seizures
3. Anticholinergic "toxidrome"
a. Hyperthermia ("hot as Hades")
b. Tachycardia
c. Hypertension
d. Hot, flushed, dry skin ("red as a beet")
e. Dilated pupils ("blind as a bat")
f. Dry mucous membranes ("dry as a bone")
g. Diminished bowel sounds
h. Urinary retention
i. Altered mental status (e.g., agitation, hallucinations) and seizures ("mad as a hatter")
4. Opioid "toxidrome"
a. Pinpoint pupils
b. Respiratory depression
c. Altered mental status (e.g., obtundation)
T O X I C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	445

Toxicologic Emergencies
b. Administration of naloxone may precipitate withdrawal in patients with
physiologic dependence to an opiate. Therefore, a lower dose such as
0.1 mg can be attempted initially to look for response.

4. Flumazenil, a benzodiazepine antagonist, is capable of reversing benzodiazepine-
induced CNS depression. The dose is 0.5 mg (to a maximum dose of 5 mg)
administered by slow intravenous push. Flumazenil has a half-life of 57 minutes,
so resedation after administration may occur within 1 to 2 hours and repeat
doses may be required. Flumazenil has limited applicability due to numerous risks.

a. Adverse effects include seizures, arrhythmias, induction of benzodiaz-
epine withdrawal, and increased intracranial pressure in patients with
head trauma.

b. Contraindications to the use of flumazenil include:
i. Co-ingestion of seizure-inducing agents, especially cyclic
antidepressants
ii. The presence of a seizure disorder that is being therapeutically sup-
pressed with benzodiazepines
iii. A history of chronic benzodiazepine use, suspected benzodiazepine
dependence, or potential benzodiazepine withdrawal
iv. Evidence on physical examination of anticholinergic or sympathomi-
metic "toxidromes"
E. Gastric decontamination procedures
1. Activated charcoal decreases the systemic absorption of many drugs and
should be administered once airway patency has been assured. The initial dose
is generally 1 g/kg.

2. Gastric lavage, in general, has no advantage over activated charcoal in terms
of preventing the absorption of toxins. Gastric lavage is appropriate in certain
situations and should be considered early in the presentation but only when
the airway is secure. Consider gastric lavage for:
a. Large, life-threatening ingestions of medications or toxins for which there is
no antidote or which impair gastrointestinal motility
b. Life-threatening ingestions of medications or toxins that are not adsorbed to
activated charcoal
3. Whole-bowel irrigation. Administration of polyethylene glycol solutions at
appropriate rates (2 L/hour in adults and 0.5 L/hour in children) speeds the
transit of substances through the gastrointestinal tract, thus decreasing their
systemic absorption. Unlike cathartics (e.g., magnesium sulfate), whole-bowel
irrigation is not associated with any adverse concomitant fluid/electrolyte
shifts. Decontamination by whole-bowel irrigation may be of benefit in several
cases, once a patent airway has been secured:
a. Life-threatening ingestions of sustained-release drugs
b. Life-threatening ingestions of medications or toxins that are not adsorbed
by activated charcoal
c. Ingestion of drug vials, bags, or packets
F. 	Laboratory and diagnostic studies. There are several quick studies that can help
isolate the toxicologic agent.

1. Laboratory studies
a. Urinary fluorescence. When urine in a plastic container is exposed to ultra-
violet light, fluorescence may indicate the presence of fluorescein, a marker
for ethylene glycol, in the urine.

b. Urinalysis. The presence of crystals, specifically calcium oxalate, may help
confirm ethylene glycol ingestion.

c. Blood color. Blood that is chocolate brown in color has at least a 15% met-
hemoglobin concentration.

d. Toxicology screens. Screening capabilities vary among laboratories.

i. Urine benzodiazepine screens. False-negative results may occur with
benzodiazepines that are not metabolized to oxazepam (e.g., clonaz-
epam, alprazolam) or in the presence of oxaprozin, a nonsteroidal anti-
inflammatory drug.

Quick 	HIT
There are a few substances
that are not adsorbed, or are
poorly adsorbed, by activated
charcoal: alcohols, lithium,
iron, lead, hydrocarbons, and
caustics. Furthermore, in the
case of a caustic ingestion,
administration of charcoal
will limit endoscopic evalua-
tion of mucosal injury.

446 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Toxicologic Emergencies
ii. Urine opiate screens assay for morphine and codeine.

a) False-positive results may occur if the patient has ingested foods
containing a high concentration of poppy seeds.

b) Fentanyl and its analogs are not detected on most urine opiate
screens, causing false-negative results after their use.

iii. Quantitative serum drug levels are certainly more helpful than qualita-
tive urine testing in directing clinical emergency management in the fol-
lowing situations:
a) Acetaminophen overdose
b) Salicylate overdose
c) Theophylline overdose
d) Lithium overdose
e) Lead poisoning
f) Carbon monoxide poisoning
g) Methemoglobinemia
h) Alcohol toxicity
i) 	Digoxin toxicity
2. Diagnostic studies
a. Electrocardiography. An electrocardiogram (ECG) is an easy way to evalu-
ate drug-induced rhythm disturbances, conduction abnormalities, and
axis changes.

b. Radiography. The radiopacity of tablets or capsules in the stomach depends
on several variables, including the size of the patient, the arrangement of
pills (i.e., bezoars), the presence of air around or in the pill, the presence
of an enteric coating, and the time since ingestion. The following are
often radiopaque:
i. Halogenated hydrocarbons
ii. Iron-containing preparations
iii. Potassium preparations
iv. Iodinated compounds
v. Heroin or cocaine packets
```
